title: Vanity Fair
author: William Makepeace Thackeray

timeline.span: c.1813–1830: two girls out of school, the road to Waterloo, and the long after

overture: Two girls leave school together and part at the gate - Becky Sharp, penniless and clever, to claw her way up through governessing and marriage into Mayfair and a scandal; and gentle, moneyed Amelia Sedley, to be ruined, widowed at Waterloo, and to endure. Their lives cross London, the field of Waterloo, and the Continent, and meet again, at the last, in a little German court - a novel without a hero, and a whole world in it.

sources.name: Project Gutenberg #599 - the full text; place-names and quotes verified verbatim
Real geography (Russell Square, Chiswick Mall, Brighton, Ostend, Brussels, Waterloo); the fictional Queen's Crawley and Pumpernickel flagged, the latter long identified with Weimar

characters.name: Becky Sharp
characters.role: Penniless, brilliant and unscrupulous; she claws her way from a charity pupil to the drawing-rooms of Mayfair and the presence of the King, and falls as fast - the engine of the book, and its rising line

characters.name: Amelia Sedley
characters.role: Gentle, moneyed and soft-hearted; ruined by her father's bankruptcy, widowed at Waterloo, and left to endure a long genteel poverty - the book's enduring line, set against Becky's rise

characters.name: George Osborne
characters.role: Amelia's vain, dashing husband, who marries her against his father's will and is killed at Waterloo weeks later - a line that runs to the battlefield and stops

locations.name: Chiswick Mall
locations.note: Chiswick Mall is real; the academy is fictional but explicitly sited there.
locations.story: Miss Pinkerton's genteel academy on the real riverside street of Chiswick Mall, where the book opens: Becky and Amelia are turned out into the world, and Becky flings the presentation dictionary back into the garden.
locations.quote: So much for the Dixonary; and, thank God, I'm out of Chiswick.

image.caption: Chiswick Mall from the Thames, 1829 - the riverside terrace where Miss Pinkerton's academy stood, its church and the Red Lion named along the front.
image.credit: From Samuel Leigh's panorama of the Thames, 1829 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Russell Square
locations.story: The Bloomsbury square where the wealthy Sedleys and, facing them, the Osbornes both live - the comfortable world Amelia is born into and loses when her father is ruined, and to which she gives up her son.
locations.imageBlank: No contemporaneous view of Russell Square itself came to hand; the Sedleys' Bloomsbury house keeps its pin rather than borrow a generic square.

locations.name: Queen's Crawley, Hampshire
locations.note: Best guess. Queen's Crawley and its villages (Mudbury, Squashmore) are fictional; the text fixes only 'Hants', within reach of Southampton. Placed in central Hampshire and flagged conjectured.
locations.story: Sir Pitt Crawley's crumbling Hampshire seat, where Becky goes as governess and lays the foundations of her fortune - charming the old baronet and secretly marrying his son Rawdon. Wholly invented; the text places it in Hampshire, near a Southampton doctor.

image.caption: Bramshill House, Hampshire - a great Jacobean seat of the kind imagined for Sir Pitt Crawley's shabby-grand old hall. Indicative; Queen's Crawley is invented.
image.credit: Drawn by J. P. Neale, from Views of the Seats of Noblemen and Gentlemen (1818) · British Library · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Brighton
locations.story: The seaside resort of George and Amelia's honeymoon, where Becky and Rawdon join them - until Dobbin brings the order that sends the regiment abroad, and the whole party for the war.

image.caption: The Chain Pier at Brighton, painted by John Constable in 1826–27 - the fashionable seafront where Rawdon and Becky take their honeymoon.
image.credit: John Constable, Chain Pier, Brighton (1826–27) · Tate · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Curzon Street, Mayfair
locations.note: Curzon Street is real Mayfair; the house number is invented.
locations.story: Becky and Rawdon's married house in Mayfair, the height of her rise - 'living well on nothing a year', received at Court, the favourite of Lord Steyne - and the scene of the scandal that brings it all down.
locations.imageBlank: No verifiable period view of Curzon Street itself; Becky's Mayfair address keeps its pin and its paragraph rather than a borrowed street scene.

locations.name: Fulham
locations.story: The modest cottage where the widowed Amelia lives out her years of genteel poverty with her parents and little Georgy, before the son is given up to old Osborne's wealth.

image.caption: Fulham, etched by James McNeill Whistler c.1879 - the old wooden bridge and All Saints' tower on the Thames, near Amelia's genteel poverty.
image.credit: James McNeill Whistler, Fulham (c.1879) · Library of Congress · public domain

locations.name: Ostend
locations.story: The Belgian port where the packet lands the regiment and its followers, on the road to the war - Ghent and Bruges beyond it, and Brussels at the end.

image.caption: The harbour at Ostend in a squall, Andreas Achenbach, 1858 - the Belgian packet port on the road to Brussels.
image.credit: Andreas Achenbach, The Harbour at Ostend (1858) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Brussels
locations.story: Where the army gathers and the English wait - the Duchess of Richmond's ball on the eve, the guns heard in the night, and Amelia praying through the day of the battle a few miles to the south.

image.caption: The Grand-Place, Brussels, photographed c.1875 - the city filling with English visitors on the eve of Waterloo.
image.credit: Anonymous photograph, c.1875 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Waterloo (Mont-Saint-Jean)
locations.note: The battlefield at Mont-Saint-Jean, Braine-l'Alleud; the Lion's Mound marker was raised 1820–26, after the battle.
locations.story: The field south of Brussels where the battle is fought on 18 June 1815 - and where George Osborne is killed, a bullet through his heart, in the book's most famous line.
locations.quote: Amelia was praying for George, who was lying on his face, dead, with a bullet through his heart.

image.caption: The field of Waterloo, drawn on the spot in 1816 - the quiet farmland at Mont-Saint-Jean a year after the battle that widows Amelia in a sentence.
image.credit: Robert Hills, from Sketches in Flanders and Holland (1816) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Boulogne-sur-Mer
locations.story: The French coast where Becky begins her long Continental exile after the scandal - 'Queen of British Boulogne' for a season, until the old creditors and the old stories find her out and move her on.
locations.quote: the French coast at Boulogne, that refuge of so much exiled English.

image.caption: Boulogne harbour, painted by Richard Parkes Bonington c.1823 - the cheap Channel port the ruined Sedleys and old Osborne retreat to.
image.credit: Richard Parkes Bonington, Boulogne Harbour (c.1823) · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Pumpernickel (Weimar)
locations.note: Pumpernickel is fictional; placed at Weimar, the town it is generally identified with, and flagged conjectured.
locations.story: Thackeray's invented little German grand-duchy, with its court and its opera - where Amelia, Dobbin and Jos come to rest at the end, and where a sunk and shabby Becky reappears to close the account. Long identified with Weimar, where Thackeray had stayed.

image.caption: The market place at Weimar, c.1900 - the little German court-town generally taken for Thackeray's Pumpernickel, where the puppets go back into the box. Indicative.
image.credit: Postcard after a Weimar view, c.1900 · Zeno.org · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

movements.note: The two friends leave school in the Sedley carriage - Amelia in tears, Becky flinging Dr Johnson's dictionary back over the gate - and drive together into town, where Becky's first campaign, on Amelia's fat brother Jos, comes to nothing
With no fortune of her own, Becky takes a governess's place at Sir Pitt Crawley's Hampshire seat - outside seat on the Trafalgar coach in the rain - and sets about charming the whole crumbling house
movements.routeNote: West out of London by the Hampshire road - Bagshot, Basingstoke - into the country.
movements.routeSource: The stage-coach road from London into Hampshire.

movements.note: Secretly married to Rawdon Crawley, she comes up to London to begin her real ascent - a little house in Mayfair, living brilliantly on nothing a year, and climbing toward the King's own drawing-room
movements.routeNote: Back up the Hampshire road to London and Mayfair.

movements.note: The Steyne scandal breaks; Rawdon casts her off and society shuts its doors. Becky slips across the Channel to Boulogne, the first refuge of her long exile among the Continental English
movements.routeNote: Down the Thames and across the Channel to the French coast at Boulogne.

movements.note: Then the long downward drift - Dieppe, the German spa towns, cards and shabby lodgings and worse - sinking year by year, until she washes up in the little court of Pumpernickel

Married at last - George against his father's will, and disinherited for it - the young couple honeymoon at Brighton, where the Crawleys join them and the officers idle, until the order comes to embark
movements.routeNote: Down the Brighton road - Croydon, Crawley - to the sea.

movements.note: The regiment for the Continent: the whole party takes the packet across to Ostend, Jos blustering about the coming campaign, the war a rumour and an excitement not yet a horror
movements.routeNote: By packet across the Channel to Ostend.
movements.routeSource: The Ostend packet (the heroines cross by Ramsgate; the transports down the river).

movements.note: On through Bruges and Ghent to Brussels, filling with troops and gaiety - the shops, the parks, the Duchess of Richmond's ball - the last brilliant days before the guns
movements.routeNote: Overland by Bruges and Ghent to Brussels.
movements.routeSource: The road from Ostend to Brussels.

movements.note: The drums beat in the night; the army marches out south to meet Napoleon. Amelia clings to George and he goes - and by the end of that day, a few miles off at Mont-Saint-Jean, he is lying dead on the field, a bullet through his heart
Widowed, and carrying George's child, Amelia is brought home to England - down at last to a small cottage at Fulham and years of gentle, grinding poverty
Long after, faithful Dobbin home from India carries the little party - Amelia, Jos, and Georgy - up the Rhine to the quiet court of Pumpernickel, where Amelia will at last see Dobbin's worth

story.title: Two girls leave school
story.narration: Two girls finish at Miss Pinkerton's academy on Chiswick Mall: gentle, wealthy Amelia Sedley, wept over by all, and poor, sharp, penniless Becky Sharp, who leaves with nothing but her wits - and flings the school's parting gift, a dictionary, back into the garden as the carriage pulls away.

story.title: To Russell Square
story.narration: They drive into London together, Becky a guest in the Sedleys' rich house on Russell Square. Her first campaign is on Amelia's vain, fat brother Jos - but it comes to nothing, and with no fortune to fall back on she must go out and earn her bread.

story.title: Becky's way up
story.narration: Follow Becky first. Without money or family, the only door open to her is service - a governess's place in the country, and the long game of making herself indispensable.

story.title: Governess at Queen's Crawley
story.narration: At Sir Pitt Crawley's tumbledown Hampshire hall she charms everyone - the coarse old baronet, his rich sister, his son. And quietly, cleverly, she marries that son, Rawdon Crawley, a guardsman and his aunt's favourite - a secret that will cost him the aunt's fortune.

story.title: The rise
story.narration: Married, she comes up to London to begin the ascent she was born for: a little house in Mayfair, living dazzlingly 'on nothing a year', and climbing, charm by charm and debt by debt, toward presentation at the King's own court.
Now wind back, and turn to Amelia. While Becky schemed in Hampshire, ruin came to Russell Square: her father Sedley is bankrupt, and the match with George Osborne all but broken.

story.title: Married, and to the war
story.narration: George marries her - pushed to it by his friend Dobbin, who loves Amelia himself but cannot bear to see her jilted - and is disinherited for it. They honeymoon at Brighton, the officers idling by the sea, until Dobbin brings the order that the regiment sails for the Continent.

story.title: Across the Channel
story.narration: The whole party takes the packet across to Ostend, Jos loud about the coming campaign, the war still a thrilling rumour rather than a horror.

story.title: Brussels, before the battle
story.narration: On through Bruges and Ghent to Brussels - the city filling with troops and gaiety, the shops and the parks and the Duchess of Richmond's great ball: the last brilliant nights before the guns.

story.title: Waterloo
story.narration: In the night the drums beat and the army marches south. Amelia clings to George; he goes. And by the end of that long day, a few miles off at Mont-Saint-Jean, he is lying on his face on the field, dead, a bullet through his heart - while in Brussels she prays for a man already gone.

story.title: The widow of Fulham
story.narration: Widowed at twenty, and carrying George's child, Amelia is brought home to England - and down, as the Sedleys sink, to a small cottage at Fulham and long years of gentle poverty, her whole heart poured into her boy.
Turn back to Becky, at the very top of the wheel while Amelia is at the bottom of it - mistress of Mayfair, wit of the town, the plaything and favourite of the great Lord Steyne.

story.title: The fall
story.narration: Then it breaks. Rawdon, arrested for debt and freed, comes home to find her alone with Steyne and the diamonds - and casts her off. The scandal empties every drawing-room in London. In an afternoon the whole glittering structure is gone.

story.title: Exile
story.narration: Cast out, Becky slips across the Channel to Boulogne - 'Queen of British Boulogne' for a season among the exiled English, until the old debts and the old stories catch her up and move her on.

story.title: The wandering years
story.narration: And then the long descent: Dieppe, the German spa towns, the card-tables and the shabby inns, sinking year by year - until she fetches up, faded and disreputable, in the little court of Pumpernickel.

And so the two threads, parted at the school gate a lifetime ago, run together one last time. Faithful Dobbin, home from India, brings Amelia and her brother abroad - and up the Rhine to the same quiet town.

story.title: Up the Rhine
story.narration: At Pumpernickel Amelia, Jos and Dobbin come to rest, and - with a hard word from Becky, of all people, about the George she has mourned so long - Amelia at last turns to the man who has loved her for twenty years.

story.title: The play played out
story.narration: There the account closes: Becky sunk and shabby but surviving, Amelia quietly happy at last, Jos dead under Becky's shadow. Which of us is happy in this world, the showman asks - and shuts the puppets back in their box, for the play is played out.
